{"path": "workspace/security_comparison.txt", "content": "================================================================================
COMPARISON: ZERO TRUST SECURITY vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
    A modern security model based on the principle of "never trust, always
    verify." It assumes that threats exist both outside and inside the
    traditional network perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or system
    should be trusted by default — even if already inside the network.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
    A layered security strategy that employs multiple, overlapping defensive
    controls to protect systems and data. The idea is that if one layer fails,
    the next layer continues to provide protection. It originates from
    military strategy and has been a foundational cybersecurity concept for
    decades.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
    • Verify Explicitly — Always authenticate and authorize based on all
      available data points (user identity, location, device health, service
      or workload, data classification, and anomalies).
    • Least Privilege Access — Limit user access with Just-In-Time (JIT) and
      Just-Enough-Access (JEA), risk-based adaptive policies, and data
      protection.
    • Assume Breach — Minimize blast radius and segment access. Use
      end-to-end encryption and continuous analytics to detect threats.
    • Micro-Segmentation — Divide the network into isolated segments so that
      lateral movement by attackers is severely restricted.
    • Continuous Monitoring — Constantly inspect and log all traffic,
      applying real-time threat detection and response.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
    • Layered Controls — Implement security at multiple levels: physical,
      network, host, application, and data.
    • Redundancy — No single control is relied upon; overlapping safeguards
      ensure resilience.
    • Diversity of Defenses — Use different types of controls (preventive,
      detective, corrective, deterrent, compensating).
    • Perimeter Focus — Traditionally emphasizes a strong outer boundary
      (firewalls, IDS/IPS) while also maintaining internal layers.
    • Assume Failure — Each layer is designed with the expectation that
      layers above or below it may be compromised.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Aspect               | Zero Trust                     | Defense in Depth              |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Trust Model           | Trust is never implicit;       | Trust can exist within the    |
|                       | every access request is        | perimeter once authenticated. |
|                       | verified regardless of origin. |                               |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Network Assumption    | The network is always hostile; | The internal network is       |
|                       | no "inside" vs. "outside."     | safer than the external one.  |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Primary Focus         | Identity-centric access        | Layered infrastructure        |
|                       | control and data protection.   | protection.                   |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Access Control        | Dynamic, risk-based, and       | Often static, role-based,     |
|                       | continuously evaluated.        | granted at perimeter entry.   |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Lateral Movement      | Actively prevented via micro-  | Slowed but not always         |
|                       | segmentation and least priv.   | prevented by design.          |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Architecture          | Identity and data form the     | The network perimeter forms   |
|                       | new perimeter.                 | the primary perimeter.        |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Historical Context    | Emerged ~2010 (Forrester);     | Rooted in ancient military    |
|                       | gained prominence with cloud   | strategy; formalized in IT    |
|                       | and remote work.               | security in the 1990s–2000s.  |


4. HOW THEY COMPLEMENT EACH OTHER
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

These two approaches are not mutually exclusive. In fact, they work best
when combined:

    • Zero Trust can be seen as a guiding philosophy that sits on top of a
      Defense in Depth implementation. The layered controls of Defense in
      Depth provide the "how," while Zero Trust provides the "why."

    • Defense in Depth ensures that multiple barriers exist (firewalls,
      encryption, endpoint protection, physical security), while Zero Trust
      ensures that even

COMPARISON: ZERO TRUST SECURITY vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
    A modern security model based on the principle of "never trust, always
    verify." It assumes that threats exist both outside and inside the
    traditional network perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or system
    should be trusted by default — even if already inside the network.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
    A layered security strategy that employs multiple, overlapping defensive
    controls to protect systems and data. The idea is that if one layer fails,
    the next layer continues to provide protection. It originates from
    military strategy and has been a foundational cybersecurity concept for
    decades.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
    • Verify Explicitly — Always authenticate and authorize based on all
      available data points (user identity, location, device health, service
      or workload, data classification, and anomalies).
    • Least Privilege Access — Limit user access with Just-In-Time (JIT) and
      Just-Enough-Access (JEA), risk-based adaptive policies, and data
      protection.
    • Assume Breach — Minimize blast radius and segment access. Use
      end-to-end encryption and continuous analytics to detect threats.
    • Micro-Segmentation — Divide the network into isolated segments so that
      lateral movement by attackers is severely restricted.
    • Continuous Monitoring — Constantly inspect and log all traffic,
      applying real-time threat detection and response.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
    • Layered Controls — Implement security at multiple levels: physical,
      network, host, application, and data.
    • Redundancy — No single control is relied upon; overlapping safeguards
      ensure resilience.
    • Diversity of Defenses — Use different types of controls (preventive,
      detective, corrective, deterrent, compensating).
    • Perimeter Focus — Traditionally emphasizes a strong outer boundary
      (firewalls, IDS/IPS) while also maintaining internal layers.
    • Assume Failure — Each layer is designed with the expectation that
      layers above or below it may be compromised.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Aspect               | Zero Trust                     | Defense in Depth              |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Trust Model           | Trust is never implicit;       | Trust can exist within the    |
|                       | every access request is        | perimeter once authenticated. |
|                       | verified regardless of origin. |                               |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Network Assumption    | The network is always hostile; | The internal network is       |
|                       | no "inside" vs. "outside."     | safer than the external one.  |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Primary Focus         | Identity-centric access        | Layered infrastructure        |
|                       | control and data protection.   | protection.                   |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Access Control        | Dynamic, risk-based, and       | Often static, role-based,     |
|                       | continuously evaluated.        | granted at perimeter entry.   |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Lateral Movement      | Actively prevented via micro-  | Slowed but not always         |
|                       | segmentation and least priv.   | prevented by design.          |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Architecture          | Identity and data form the     | The network perimeter forms   |
|                       | new perimeter.                 | the primary perimeter.        |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Historical Context    | Emerged ~2010 (Forrester);     | Rooted in ancient military    |
|                       | gained prominence with cloud   | strategy; formalized in IT    |
|                       | and remote work.               | security in the 1990s–2000s.  |


4. HOW THEY COMPLEMENT EACH OTHER
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

These two approaches are not mutually exclusive. In fact, they work best
when combined:

    • Zero Trust can be seen as a guiding philosophy that sits on top of a
      Defense in Depth implementation. The layered controls of Defense in
      Depth provide the "how," while Zero Trust provides the "why."

    • Defense in Depth ensures that multiple barriers exist (firewalls,
      encryption, endpoint protection, physical security), while Zero Trust
      ensures that even if an attacker breaches one layer, they cannot
      automatically access everything behind it.

    • Together, they create a robust security posture: Defense in Depth
      provides breadth (covering all attack surfaces), and Zero Trust
      provides depth (ens
